title: LRU Cache
code: public void Put(int key, int value)

dll.label: doubly linked list (head = MRU, tail = LRU)

frames.caption: Start: capacity 2, empty cache. Two sentinels (head=H, tail=T) bracket the list so insert/remove never see a null neighbour. We will run Put(1,1), Put(2,2), Get(1), Put(3,3), Get(2).
frames.note: An LRU cache = a hash map (key -> node, O(1) lookup) PLUS a doubly linked list ordered by recency (head = most-recent, tail = least-recent). The map finds any node instantly; the list reorders/evicts in O(1) because each node knows both neighbours. That pairing is what makes both get and put O(1).

frames.caption: Put(1, 1): key 1 is not in the map and the map is not full (0 < 2), so create a node and insert it at the front.
frames.note: A put on a fresh key skips the update branch and the eviction branch; it just allocates a node, records key -> node in the map, and InsertFront makes it the new head (most recent).

frames.caption: Insert node 1:1 at the front. _map[1] = node; head -> 1 -> tail. Node 1 is now the MRU.
frames.note: InsertFront splices the node in just after the head sentinel: n.next = head.next; n.prev = head; head.next.prev = n; head.next = n. Four pointer writes, all O(1) — no scan, because the hash map handed us the exact node. The head sentinel means "insert at front" never has to special-case an empty list.

frames.caption: Put(2, 2): key 2 not in the map, map holds 1 entry (1 < 2), still room — create node 2:2 and insert at front.
frames.note: Same path as Put(1,1): no update, no eviction. After InsertFront the order becomes 2 (MRU), 1 (LRU) — the most recently written key sits right behind the head sentinel.

frames.caption: Insert node 2:2 at the front. Order is now 2 -> 1 (MRU..LRU). The map has both keys.
frames.note: InsertFront splices the node in just after the head sentinel: n.next = head.next; n.prev = head; head.next.prev = n; head.next = n. Four pointer writes, all O(1) — no scan, because the hash map handed us the exact node. The head sentinel means "insert at front" never has to special-case an empty list.

frames.caption: Get(1): the map has key 1, so it is a HIT. We must move node 1 to the front to mark it most-recently-used.
frames.note: TryGetValue returns the node in O(1) — no list scan. A hit always promotes the node to the head; that promotion is exactly how recency is maintained.

frames.caption: Remove node 1: stitch its neighbours together (1.prev.next = 1.next; 1.next.prev = 1.prev). It is now detached.
frames.note: Remove unlinks a node by stitching its neighbours together: n.prev.next = n.next; n.next.prev = n.prev. This is O(1) ONLY because the list is DOUBLY linked — we already hold n.prev. A singly linked list would have to walk from the head to find the predecessor, making eviction O(n); that is the whole reason LRU needs a doubly linked list.

frames.caption: InsertFront node 1: order is now 1 -> 2 (MRU..LRU). Return its value 1. Key 1 is freshly the MRU; key 2 is now the LRU.
frames.note: Get is two map+list moves: the hash map jumps straight to the node in O(1), then Remove + InsertFront move it to the front (most-recently-used) in O(1). Touching a key is what keeps it alive — recency is encoded purely by position in the list, head = newest, tail = oldest.

frames.caption: Put(3, 3): key 3 is not in the map, and _map.Count == _capacity (2 == 2). The cache is full, so we must evict before inserting.
frames.note: The overflow branch fires only when the key is new AND the map is at capacity. The victim is the least-recently-used node, found in O(1) as tail.prev.

frames.caption: lru = _tail.prev -> node 2:2 (key 2). It is the least-recently-used, so it is the eviction victim.
frames.note: Because the list stays recency-ordered, the LRU is always exactly one hop in front of the tail sentinel. No search needed.

frames.caption: Evict: Remove(lru) unlinks node 2 AND _map.Remove(2) drops its key. Both the list and the map forget key 2 — no dangling entry left behind.
frames.note: The map is full, so before inserting we evict the least-recently-used node — the real one is tail.prev (the tail itself is a sentinel). Unlink it AND delete its key from the map, or the map would leak a dangling entry. Because the list is recency-ordered, the victim is always one hop from the tail: O(1).

frames.caption: Now insert node 3:3 at the front. Order is 3 -> 1 (MRU..LRU); the map holds keys {3, 1}. Put(3,3) is done — still O(1) overall.
frames.note: InsertFront splices the node in just after the head sentinel: n.next = head.next; n.prev = head; head.next.prev = n; head.next = n. Four pointer writes, all O(1) — no scan, because the hash map handed us the exact node. The head sentinel means "insert at front" never has to special-case an empty list.

frames.caption: Get(2): TryGetValue(2) fails — key 2 was evicted by the previous put, so it is gone from the map.
frames.note: Get(2) misses: key 2 was evicted when Put(3,3) overflowed capacity 2, so it is no longer in the map. A miss returns -1 without touching the list. Every operation here — hit, miss, insert, evict — is O(1), which is the entire promise of the hash-map + doubly-linked-list combination.

frames.caption: Get(2) returns -1 (cache miss). Final recency order: 3 (MRU), 1 (LRU). Get(1) and Get(3) would still hit; only the evicted key 2 misses.
frames.note: This matches the reviewer quiz: after Get(1) promoted key 1, Put(3,3) evicted the tail (key 2), so Get(2) -> -1. Every step touched at most a handful of pointers: O(1) get, O(1) put, by design.
